title-group.article-title: The efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer: A meta-analysis and reconstructed individual patient data analysis

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Urology, Tangdu Hospital, Fourth Military Medical University, Xi’an, Shaanxi, China
bDepartment of Urology, Institute of Urology, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan, China
cDivision of Surgery & Interventional Science, University College London, London, UK
dBasic and Translational Cancer Research Group, Department of Pharmacology and Therapeutics, College of Medicine, University of Nigeria, Nsukka, Nigeria

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Uzoamaka Adaobi Okoli u.okoli@ucl.ac.uk Division of Surgery & Interventional Science, University College London, London, UK
Geng Zhang zuibingling@163.com Department of Urology, Tangdu Hospital, Fourth Military Medical University, No. 569 Xinsi Road, Baqiao District, Xi’an, Shaanxi, China.

fn.p: These authors contributed equally to this work.
The Fourth Military Medical University is also named Air Force Medical University.

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published with license by Taylor & Francis Group, LLC.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Some immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) has been used for men with specific metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). However, inconsistent results of ICI for unselected mCRPC population raise concerns. The primary outcomes were overall survival (OS) and radiographic progression-free survival (PFS), and the secondary outcomes included objective response rate (ORR). Individual patient data (IPD) were reconstructed from reported survival curve, and Kaplan–Meier method was used to evaluate the efficacy. Data of 1580 patients from 21 clinical trials were analyzed without biomarker-selected indications. The pooled 1-y and 2-y OS were 63% and 39%, while pooled 6-month and 12-month PFS were 41% and 17%. ICI plus other agent showed more OS and PFS benefits comparing with mono or dual ICI therapy (All P < .05). Combination therapy with ICI showed encouraging but preliminary signs in mCRPC. Future large trials focusing on combination regimens of ICI are needed.

funding-group.funding-statement: This manuscript was funded by the Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 824B2017].

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Prostate cancer (PCa) is a leading cause of cancer-related mortality in men with around 400,000 deaths worldwide each year.1,2 Combination of androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) plus androgen receptor pathway inhibitors (ARPIs) currently constitutes the standard of care for high-risk localized PCa, metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC) and metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC).3,4 Among them, mCRPC as an end stage is almost inevitable and incurable.5 In recent years, several new treatments for mCRPC have been used to delay the disease progression, such as poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitors (PARPIs), novel ARPIs and radioligand therapy.6–10 However, mCRPC is still a lethal condition due to irreversible disease progression and drug resistance regardless of initial response.11,12 Thus, clinical trials exploring the management of mCRPC are constantly increasing.

Immunotherapy is developing and transforming as an effective cancer treatment currently.13,14 Immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI), an important progression of immunotherapy that works by utilizing the host immune system to target tumor cells, has been applied in many malignancies including genitourinary and gastrointestinal cancers.15–18 For patients with mCRPC refractory to standard of care, ICI treatment as a potentially effective approach has been tested in many clinical trials over the past decade.3,19 In this regard, cytotoxic T lymphocyte antigen 4 (CTLA-4), programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1), and programmed death ligand 1 (PD-L1) inhibitors have been evaluated in mCRPC by many clinical trials.11,12 Specifically, pembrolizumab has been approved for mCRPC with microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H), mismatch-repair deficient (dMMR) or have a high tumor mutational burden (TMB).11,12,20,21

Currently, emerging evidence suggest that ICI activity in mCRPC is largely confined to biomarker-defined subsets, including tumors with MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-high status, homologous recombination repair (HRR) or broader DDR gene alterations, and CDK12 biallelic loss, which are associated with higher neoantigen burden and inflamed immune microenvironments.10–12,20,21 Meanwhile, early-phase studies have explored ICI in combination with prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA)-targeted radioligand therapy, such as a single dose of lutetium-177 (177Lu)-PSMA-617 (177Lu-PSMA-617) followed by maintenance pembrolizumab, which has shown encouraging preliminary activity and acceptable safety in men with mCRPC selected for high PSMA expression.10 However, mCRPC was considered to possess an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment, which may restrict its response to ICI.22

Although different background therapies were combined, clinical evidence on the efficacy of ICI in unselected mCRPC population was inconsistent or even controversial, which raises concerns of using ICI in mCRPC.23–25 For example, a phase III trial showed that adding atezolizumab to enzalutamide did not provide overall survival benefit compared with enzalutamide alone in men with unselected mCRPC, highlighting the challenges of translating the successful application of ICI in other solid tumors to PCa.26 Additionally, the sample sizes of most finished trials were small, which may impact the robustness and obscure potential benefits of specific ICI in subgroups. Thus, we conducted this study to explore the efficacy of ICI given as monotherapy or in combination with systemic agents excepted for sequential therapy (e.g. radioligand followed by ICI) in mCRPC using reconstructed individual-level data.

sec.title: Literature search
sec.p: This meta-analysis was conducted according to the PRISMA and AMSTAR guidelines.27,28 A comprehensive search of PubMed, Embase, and the Cochrane Library was performed from inception to June 2025. The strategy combined terms related to the disease and interventions, including: (“Prostate cancer” OR “Metastatic prostate cancer” OR “Castration-resistant prostate cancer”) AND (“Immune checkpoint inhibitor” OR “Checkpoint blockade” OR “PD-1” OR “PD-L1” OR “CTLA-4”)(Detailed searching terms showed in supplementary file 1). Ongoing or completed trials were additionally screened on ClinicalTrials.gov. No restrictions were placed on backgrounddrugs or combination regimens. Records of included studies were managed in EndNote, and two reviewers (ZL and YX) independently screened titles/abstracts and full texts, with disagreements resolved by discussion or a third reviewer (WZ). The protocol was registered on PROSPERO (CRD420251087847).

sec.title: Literature selection
sec.p: Studies were eligible when they met all of the following criteria: (a) histologically diagnosed prostate adenocarcinoma; (b) documented metastatic castration-resistant status; (c) prospective clinical trials or randomized controlled trials (RCTs); (d) phase I-III trials that had at least one arm receiving ICI regardless of type and dose; (e) ICI as monotherapy or in combination with systemic agents such as ARPIs, chemotherapy, PARPIs or vaccines except for ICI-radioligand sequential therapy; (f) time-to-event outcomes were available via Kaplan–Meier curves or reported estimates; (g) published by English language. We excluded retrospective studies, reviews, commentaries, case reports, letters, and reports lacking survival information. When multiple reports originated from the same trial, the most recent data was preferred.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: This study was conducted based on intention-to-treat (ITT) basis. Publication information, baseline characteristics of patients, ICI type and target, intervention protocols, ITT sizes, event ratios and survival curves were extracted from studies. All data from most recent clinical trials were extracted. When final outcomes were not reported, those from prior version of the same trial were extracted. If the survival rates or event counts were not reported but the survival curve provided, survival rates were extracted from the curve, and the event counts were estimated according to rates and ITT population.

The estimated event counts based on ITT population were used when reported data was adjusted for censored cases. When a study included both ICI arm and combination arm of ICI plus non-medicine treatment (e.g. radiotherapy), only the data of ICI arm was extracted. If the survival curves of the overall population were not described, it were reconstructed by merging available results of subgroups. If both two arms containing ICI therapy without background chemotherapy, the data from two arms were pooled as the ICI arm. If the protocols included immunotherapy before and after chemotherapy, only the data from arm with sequential immunotherapy after chemotherapy was extracted. When RCT was included, only the data of ICI arm was used. Two authors (LW and QX) independently reviewed and extracted the data, while two additional authors (WZ and QF) were designated to re-check these data, in which any discrepancies were resolved by consensus.

sec.title: IPD reconstruction
sec.p: When individual patient data (IPD) were unavailable, IPD were reconstructed from published Kaplan-Meier curves using the inverted Kaplan-Meier approach, as previously reported.29,30 Survival plots were digitized with Engauge Digitizer (version 12.1) to extract time-survival coordinates at fine intervals, and numbers-at-risk were recorded at all reported landmarks. We then used the IPDfromKM package to simulate event times by integrating coordinates, total events, and risk tables, thereby approximating the underlying survival distribution for downstream analyses.31

sec.title: Outcomes and quality assessment
sec.p: The primary outcomes were overall survival (OS) and radiographic progression-free survival (rPFS). Secondary outcomes included objective response rate (ORR), treatment-related adverse events (TRAEs), and immune-related adverse events (irAEs). ORR was evaluated among patients with measurable disease, prioritizing assessments per RECIST v1.1 where available. Risk of bias was independently assessed by two reviewers using RoB 2 for randomized trials and ROBINS-I for non-randomized studies, across their prespecified domains, and judgments were classified as low risk, some concerns, or high risk, with discrepancies resolved by consensus.32,33

sec.title: Subgroup and sensitivity analysis
sec.p: We conducted prespecified subgroup meta-analysis according to treatment regimen (ICI monotherapy, ICI plus other agent, and dual ICI therapy), background therapy within combination regimens and ICI target (PD-1/PD-L1, CTLA-4, or CTLA-4 plus PD-1). In addition, we stratified results by trial phase and design (single-arm versus randomized trials) to examine the impact of study design on pooled estimates when appropriate. Sensitivity analysis was performed by omitting each study in turn (leave-one-out analyses) to assess the influence of individual trials on the pooled estimates. In this process, we excluded studies with a total sample size less than 30 to mitigate small-study effects.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: All analyses followed the intention-to-treat principle where applicable. For binary endpoints, we pooled logit-transformed proportions using inverse-variance weighting under fixed- and random-effects models. Between-study variance (τ2) was estimated by REML, and 95% prediction intervals were reported when appropriate. Heterogeneity was quantified with I2 (= < 30%, 30–49%, 50–74%, ≥75% representing low, moderate, substantial, and considerable). Confidence limits for τ2 and τ were obtained via the Q-profile method. Robustness was explored through one-study-removed influence analysis.

We performed meta-regression to examine prespecified covariates (e.g., ICI target, regimen and prior therapy). Publication bias was examined by visual inspection of contour‑enhanced funnel plots. The null effect contour is defined as the contour drawn at a specified confidence level (90%, 95% and 99%) centered on the null hypothesis of no effect. Small sample size in this meta-analysis was defined as study enrolling fewer than 30 cases, which was excluded in secondary subgroup analyses and survival analyses to mitigate the influence of small-study effects and ensure precision.34 Because most endpoints included fewer than 10 studies and exhibited substantial heterogeneity, we did not conduct formal regression-based tests for funnel-plot asymmetry, such as Egger’s or Begg’s tests.

For time-to-event outcomes, we generated Kaplan–Meier estimates by ICI regimen and compared groups using log-rank tests with Benjamini–Hochberg adjustment for multiple comparisons. In secondary analyses, we fitted Cox models to estimate hazard ratios (HR) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Analyses were conducted in R 4.4.2 (packages: survival, survminer, IPDfromKM, metafor, ggplot2).

sec.title: Characteristics of the included studies
sec.p:
The flowchart of literature screening and selecting was shown in Figure 1. After duplicates removed, 596 studies were screened and then 40 full-text studies were assessed for eligibility. Finally, 21 published clinical trials were included, in which 20 trials were at phase I-II and only 1 trial at phase III. Among them, 4 trials assessed the efficacy of atezolizumab, 3 trials for ipilimumab, 4 trials for nivolumab, 4 trials for pembrolizumab, 2 trials for durvalumab, 1 trial for avelumab, 3 trials for ipilimumab plus nivolumab. The data of 1580 patients from these trials were extracted and analyzed eventually.26,35–53 The characteristics of included trials are summarized in Table 1, with additional baseline details provided in Table S1. The summary of quality assessment for included studies were demonstrated in Figure S1 and Supplementary file 2. Figure 1.
Flowchart of screening and searching literature.Table 1.
Characteristics of included studies.
StudyRegisterPublication datePublication journalDesignPhasePopulationICI TargetMedian age (year)Median follow-up (month)
Jochems C, et al.34NCT001139842014Cancer Immunol ImmunotherSingle-armPhase INo prior treatment with chemotherapy; progression on standard hormonal therapyCTLA-469 (49–81)NR
Kwek SS, et al.35No report2015Cancer Immunol ResSingle-armPhase IbNo prior treatment with steroids, chemotherapyor immunotherapyCTLA-471 (47–82)NR
Karzai F, et al.36NCT024844042018J Immunother CancerSingle-armPhase IIProgression on prior abiraterone and/or enzalutamidePD-L166 (45–79)NR
Sharma P, et al.53NCT029859572020Cancer CellSingle-arm, two cohortsPhase IIProgression on ≥ 1 ARPI inhibitor; after chemotherapyCTLA-4/PD-165 (46–84)13.5
Subudhi SK, et al.37NCT021136572020Sci Transl MedSingle-armPhase IIProgression on standard hormonal therapyCTLA-468 (54–83)45.5
Antonarakis ES, et al.38NCT027870052020J Clin OncolSingle-arm, three cohortsPhase IIPreviously treated with docetaxel and targeted endocrine therapyPD-L168NR
Petrylak DP, et al.39NCT013758422021Clin Cancer ResSingle-armPhase IProgression on sipuleucel-T or enzalutamidePD-L168 (45–83)13
Dorff T, et al.40NCT030242162021J Immunother CancerSingle-armPhase IbNo prior chemotherapy, radiotherapy, immunotherapy, or hormonal therapyPD-L175 (53–86)NR
Shenderov E, et al.41NCT026010142021ProstateSingle-arm, two cohortsPhase IIAR-V7 expressing mCRPCCTLA-4/PD-167 (52–77)9.9
Agarwal N, et al.42NCT031709602022Lancet OncolSingle-armPhase IbProgression on/after abiraterone or enzalutamidePD-L170 (64–75)15.2
Powles T, et al.26NCT030163122022Nat MedRCTPhase IIIProgression on abirateronePD-L140–9215.2
Yu EY, et al.43NCT028615732022Eur UrolSingle-armPhase IbNo prior treatment with chemotherapy; progression on abiraterone or enzalutamidePD-L168 (64–74)32.4
Fizazi K, et al.44NCT033387902022Eur J CancerSingle-armPhase IINo prior treatment with chemotherapyPD-171 (53–88)15.2
Stein MN, et al.45NCT023255572022OncologistSingle-armPhase I/IIProgression after ≤2 prior systemic treatment regimensPD-L168 (45–92)NR

Heath EI, et al.50NCT041598962024Invest New DrugsSingle-armPhase IIAR-positive; progression on abiraterone or enzalutamide and chemotherapyPD-173 (61–82)NR
Isaacsson Velho P, et al.51NCT030407912024Clin Cancer ResSingle-armPhase IIPrior docetaxel therapyPD-168 (46–82)31.2

Please note: Details of included studies were attached in Supplementary file.
AR-V7: Androgen receptor splice variant 7; ARPI: Androgen receptor pathway inhibitor; CRPC: Castration-resistant prostate cancer; CTLA-4: Cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4; ICI: Immune checkpoint inhibitor; irAE: Immune-related adverse event; NR: Not reported; PD-1: Programmed cell death protein 1; PD-L1: Programmed death ligand 1; RCT: Randomized controlled trial; TRAE: Treatment-related adverse event.

sec.title: Assessment of efficacy by OS in the overall population and subgroups
sec.p:
The pooled 1-y OS and 6-month PFS were used to assess the efficacy of ICI. The estimates of 2-y OS and 12-month PFS were also pooled to explore the efficacy of long-term survival benefits. The pooled 1-y and 2-y OS rates were 63% (ES: 0.63, 95% CI: 0.55–0.70, I2 = 79.0%, P < .0001) and 39% (ES: 0.39, 95% CI: 0.27–0.51, I2 = 88.5%, P < .0001) respectively (Figure 2A; Figure S2A). In subgroup analyses, the pooled 1-y OS rates in patients with ICI monotherapy, ICI plus other agent (hormone therapy, chemotherapy, targeted therapy or vaccine) and dual ICI therapy were 52% (ES: 0.52, 95% CI: 0.46–0.57, I2 = 1.9%, P = .3826, n = 361), 70% (ES: 0.70, 95% CI: 0.61–0.78, I2 = 76.3%, P < .0001, n = 1028) and 45% (ES: 0.45, 95% CI: 0.35–0.55, I2 = 46.3%, P = .1554, n = 122) respectively (Figure 2A). The considerable heterogeneity was detected, which was mainly attributed to the subgroup with ICI plus other agent (I2 = 76.3%). Figure 2.
Forest plots showing the pooled 1-y OS in the overall population and subgroups stratified by treatment protocol (A); Forest plots showing the pooled 1-y. OS in patients with combination treatment stratified by background therapy (B).Abbreviations: CI: Confidence intervals; ICI: Immune checkpoint inhibitor; OS: Overall survival.

After removing studies with small sample size (<30 cases), further subgroup analyses in patients with ICI plus other agent showed that the pooled 1-y OS of ICI plus hormone therapy, chemotherapy, targeted therapy or vaccine were 0.71 (ES: 0.71, 95% CI: 0.53–0.84, I2 = 86.5%, P = .0066, n = 424), 0.73 (ES: 0.73, 95% CI: 0.66–0.79, I2 = 10.6%, P = .2901, n = 188), 0.63 (ES: 0.63, 95% CI: 0.54–0.71, I2 = 73.0%, P = .0544, n = 234) and 0.81 (ES: 0.81, 95% CI: 0.73–0.87, I2 = 27.1%, P = .2493, n = 146), respectively (Figure 2B). The differences between subgroups were significant (P = .0164). Additionally, ICI monotherapy showed consistent 2-y OS with low heterogeneity (ES: 0.41, 95% CI: 0.32–0.50, I2 = 0.0%, P = .4775, n = 103) (Figure S2A). Sensitivity analyses for pooled 1-y OS revealed consistent heterogeneity when any single trial was excluded, and for pooled 2-y OS showed increased heterogeneity when only RCT (Powles T, et al. 2022) was excluded (Figure S3).

sec.title: Assessment of efficacy by PFS in the overall population and subgroups
sec.p:
The pooled 6-month and 12-month PFS rates were 41% (ES: 0.41, 95% CI: 0.31–0.52, I2 = 91.3%, P < .0001) and 17% (ES: 0.17, 95% CI: 0.12–0.24, I2 = 81.5%, P < .0001) respectively (Figure 3A; Figure S2B). The pooled 6-month PFS rates in patients with ICI monotherapy, ICI plus other agent and dual ICI therapy were 21% (ES: 0.21, 95% CI: 0.12–0.33, I2 = 77.6%, P = .0114, n = 323), 49% (ES: 0.49, 95% CI: 0.39–0.59, I2 = 85.6%, P < .0001, n = 973) and 27% (ES: 0.27, 95% CI: 0.15–0.42, only one trial in this subgroup) respectively (Figure 3A). The considerable heterogeneity were detected in two subgroups, and further subgroup analyses were conducted in patients with ICI plus other agent. Figure 3.
Forest plots showing the pooled 6-month PFS in the overall population and subgroups stratified by treatment protocol (A); Forest plots showing the pooled 6-month PFS in patients with combination treatment stratified by background therapy (B).Abbreviations: CI: Confidence intervals; ICI: Immune checkpoint inhibitor; PFS: Radiographic progression-free survival.

After removing studies with small sample size, subgroup analyses indicated that the pooled 6-month PFS of ICI plus hormone therapy, chemotherapy, targeted therapy or vaccine were 0.42 (ES: 0.42, 95% CI: 0.37–0.47, I2 = 0.0%, P = .5685, n = 424), 0.74 (ES: 0.74, 95% CI: 0.67–0.80, I2 = 0.0%, P = .7117, n = 188), 0.44 (ES: 0.44, 95% CI: 0.38–0.50, I2 = 0.0%, P = .5767, n = 234) and 0.30 (ES: 0.30, 95% CI: 0.20–0.41, I2 = 2.5%, P = .3111, n = 74) (Figure 3B). The pooled estimates of subgroups were significantly different (P < .0001). Moreover, the pooled 12-month PFS of ICI monotherapy and ICI plus other agent was 0.09 (ES: 0.09, 95% CI: 0.03–0.26, I2 = 88.4%, P = .0002, n = 323) and 0.21 (ES: 0.21, 95% CI: 0.15–0.28, I2 = 75.3%, P < .0001, n = 973), respectively (Figure S2B). Sensitivity analyses for pooled 6-month and 12-month PFS demonstrated consistency when omitting any single trial (Figure S4).

sec.title: Assessment of efficacy by ORR in the overall population and subgroups
sec.p:
The pooled ORR estimates were used to explore the short-term efficacy of ICI. The pooled ORR were 10% (ES: 0.10, 95% CI: 0.06–0.16, I2 = 69.9%, P < .0001) (Figure 4A). The pooled ORR in subgroups of ICI monotherapy, ICI plus other agent and dual ICI therapy were 5% (ES: 0.05, 95% CI: 0.01–0.18, I2 = 75.8%, P = .0062, n = 276), 13% (ES: 0.13, 95% CI: 0.07–0.22, I2 = 69.1%, P = .0006, n = 580) and 10% (ES: 0.10, 95% CI: 0.06–0.17, I2 = 0.0%, P = .9751, n = 117) respectively (Figure 4A). The differences between subgroups were insignificant (P = .4297). Substantial to considerable heterogeneity were determined in subgroups of ICI monotherapy and ICI plus other agent. Figure 4.
Forest plots showing the pooled ORR in the overall population and subgroups stratified by treatment protocol (A); Forest plots showing the pooled ORR in patients with combination treatment stratified by background therapy (B).Abbreviations: CI: Confidence intervals; ICI: Immune checkpoint inhibitor; ORR: Objective response rate.

Further subgroup analyses were performed in patients with ICI plus other agent after studies with small sample size removed. The pooled ORR in subgroups of ICI plus hormone therapy, chemotherapy, targeted therapy or vaccine were 16% (ES: 0.16, 95% CI: 0.09–0.25, I2 = 69.1%, P = .0721, n = 173), 31% (ES: 0.31, 95% CI: 0.20–0.44, I2 = 68.5%, P = .0748, n = 97), 16% (ES: 0.16, 95% CI: 0.07–0.30, I2 = 81.8%, P = .0189, n = 191), 3% (ES: 0.03, 95% CI: 0.01–0.10, I2 = 0.0%, P = .9996, n = 79), respectively (Figure 4B). The pooled estimates of ORR between subgroups were significantly different (P = .0014). Sensitivity analyses indicated consistent estimates of pooled ORR when omitting any single trial (Figure S5).

sec.title: Survival analysis of reconstructed IPD data
sec.p:
Kaplan–Meier curves were plotted to visualize the survival characteristics and compare the survival benefits of different ICI using reconstructed IPD. The median OS in all treated patients was 15.7 (14.7–17.1) months (Figure 5A). Meanwhile, the median OS in patients treated with atezolizumab, ipilimumab, nivolumab and pembrolizumab were 15.9 (95% CI: 14.7–17.3) months, 25.7 (95% CI: 21.7–35.2) months, 17.9 (95% CI: 16.5–24.5) months and 14.4 (95% CI: 13.4–16.2) months respectively (Figure 5B-E). On the other hand, the median PFS in all treated patients was 4.6 (95% CI: 4.3–5.0) months. For patients treated with atezolizumab, nivolumab and pembrolizumab, the median PFS were 4.6 (95% CI: 4.5–5.5) months, 7.7 (95% CI: 6.0–8.6) months and 4.0 (95% CI: 3.7–4.3) months, respectively. For trials in which median OS or PFS and/or HRs were reported, the medians and effect estimates obtained from the reconstructed IPD were consistent with those reported in the original publications, and no substantial discrepancies that would alter clinical interpretation were observed. Figure 5.
Kaplan-Meier plots of OS in the overall population (A), atezolizumab group (B), ipilimumab group (C), nivolumab group (D) and pembrolizumab group (E); Kaplan-Meier plots of PFS in the overall population (G), atezolizumab group (H), nivolumab group (I) and pembrolizumab group (J).Abbreviations: CI: Confidence intervals; ICI: Immune checkpoint inhibitor; OS: Overall survival; PFS: Radiographic progression-free survival.

For subgroups of ICI monotherapy, ICI plus other agent and dual ICI therapy, Kaplan-Meier curves showed significant OS difference when comparing ICI plus other agent with monotherapy and dual ICI therapy (All P < .001), and no difference between ICI monotherapy and dual ICI therapy (P = .400) (Figure 6A). Similarly, significant PFS differences were also observed between subgroups of ICI plus other agent with monotherapy (P < .001) or dual ICI therapy (P = .007) (Figure 6B). Meanwhile, PFS in subgroups of ICI monotherapy and dual ICI therapy had no significant difference (P = .072) (Figure 6B). Figure 6.
Kaplan-Meier plots of OS (A) and PFS (B) in subgroups stratified by treatment protocol; Kaplan-Meier plots of OS (C) and PFS (D) in subgroups stratified by background therapy; Kaplan-Meier plots of OS (E) and PFS (F) in subgroups stratified by ICI type.Abbreviations: BH: Benjamini–Hochberg method; CI: Confidence intervals; ICI: Immune checkpoint inhibitor; OS: Overall survival; PFS: Radiographic progression-free survival.

In patients treated with ICI plus other agent, ICI plus vaccine demonstrated more OS benefits than combination of ICI with hormone therapy (P < .001), chemotherapy (P = .013) and targeted therapy (P < .001) (Figure 6C). Meanwhile, ICI plus chemotherapy showed better OS than hormone therapy (P < .001) and targeted therapy (P = .012) (Figure 6C). For PFS, ICI plus chemotherapy showed more benefits than the other three combination therapies (All P < .001)(Figure 6D). In patients with different ICI treatment, ipilimumab revealed more OS benefits than atezolizumab (P < .001), nivolumab (P = .025) and pembrolizumab (P < .001) (Figure 6E). Moreover, nivolumab treatment showed better PFS than atezolizumab and pembrolizumab (All P < .001) (Figure 6F). We also explored the survival benefits of different ICI targets (CTLA-4, PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibitors), and found that ICI only targeting CTLA-4 showed better OS than targeting PD-1/PD-L1 and CTLA-4 plus PD-1 (All P < .001) (Figure S6A). The PFS benefits of targeting CTLA-4 tended to be homogeneous with targeting PD-1/PD-L1 (P = .031) and CTLA-4 plus PD-1 (P = .173) (Figure S6B).

sec.title: The safety of ICI in the overall population and subgroups
sec.p: Any grade TRAEs, grade ≥3 TRAEs and any grade irAEs were used to evaluate the safety of ICI. The pooled estimate of any grade AEs in the overall population is 89% (ES: 0.89, 95% CI: 0.81–0.94, I2 = 89.4%, P < .0001)(Figure S7). The pooled estimates of any grade AEs in subgroups of ICI monotherapy, ICI plus other agent and dual ICI therapy were 71% (ES: 0.71, 95% CI: 0.51–0.85, I2 = 80.4%, P = .0061, n = 331), 92% (ES: 0.92, 95% CI: 0.86–0.96, I2 = 82.7%, P < .0001, n = 901) and 88% (ES: 0.88, 95% CI: 0.63–0.97, I2 = 84.1%, P = .0122, n = 92), respectively (Figure S7). The subgroup differences were significant (P = .0152).

The pooled estimate of grade ≥3 TRAEs and any grade irAEs were 39% (ES: 0.39, 95% CI: 0.29–0.50, I2 = 90.4%, P < .0001) and 30% (ES: 0.30, 95% CI: 0.18–0.44, I2 = 93.2%, P < .0001) respectively (Figure S8). Despite substantial to considerable heterogeneity detected, ICI monotherapy showed lowest incidences of grade ≥3 TRAEs (ES: 0.22, 95% CI: 0.10–0.42, I2 = 90.4%, P < .0001, n = 331) and any grade irAEs (ES: 0.17, 95% CI: 0.13–0.22, I2 = 51.3%, P = .1519, n = 293)(Figure S8). Sensitivity analyses for pooled any grade TRAEs, grade ≥3 TRAEs and any grade irAEs showed consistent directions and intervals of estimates when any single trial was excluded (Figure S9).

sec.title: Meta-regression
sec.p: In this study, meta-regression analyses were conducted to evaluate the effects of four moderators (age, follow-up duration, baseline PSA level, and ICI therapeutic target) on outcomes (Table S2). Overall, age and follow-up duration did not significantly affect heterogeneity for any endpoint (All P > .05) with high residual I2, suggesting limited influence of the two variables on the effect estimates. By contrast, baseline PSA level was a significant moderator for 1-y OS (QM = 45.7705, P < .001, I2 = 14.93%), 2-y OS (QM = 7.7044, P = .006, I2 = 79.25%), and any grade TRAEs (QM = 7.3571, P = .007; I2 = 64.71%), indicating that baseline PSA was associated with variability in survival and toxicity outcomes. These findings are consistent with PSA being a surrogate of tumor burden and aggressive disease biology in mCRPC, and indicate that part of the heterogeneity in survival and safety outcomes reflects differences in baseline disease burden among the included cohorts.

Moreover, ICI therapeutic targets (PD-1/PD-L1, CTLA-4 and CTLA-4 plus PD-1) also accounted for a significant proportion of heterogeneity in 1-y OS (QM = 12.7584, P = .005, I2 = 76.45%), 2-y OS (QM = 23.8276, P < .001, I2 = 65.80%), ORR (QM = 13.5822, P = .004, I2 = 62.37%), and any grade irAEs (QM = 8.5637, P = .036, I2 = 86.76%). These results suggested that both baseline disease burden and checkpoint target help explain a proportion of the observed heterogeneity in ICI outcomes across mCRPC trials, despite of substantial residual heterogeneity remains unexplained.

sec.title: Publication bias
sec.p: Contour-enhanced funnel plot were used to explore the publication bias. Publication biases and significant heterogeneity were detected in the primarily pooled estimates of efficacy and safety (Figure S10, A, C, E, G, I, K to M). In main subgroup of ICI plus other agent after excluding studies of small sample size, outliers were decreased (Figure S10, B, D, F, H and J). Additionally, when small sample studies were excluded, pooled estimates for OS, PFS and ORR changed modestly and remained within the 95% CIs of the primary analyses (Figure S11). These findings suggest that while small studies may contribute to some degree of asymmetry, and not materially alter the overall results. Additionally, survival results from reconstructed IPD were also summarized for comparison with published primary data (Table S3).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: PCa is one of the most common cancers in men and a major cause of cancer-related death.1 The mCRPC presents a major clinical challenge due to this advanced stage inevitably develops resistance to several conventional therapies, severely limiting the treatment efficacy and patients survival.6 Although treatment options are increasing, mCRPC is still a lethal disease in most cases.54 Currently, more and more clinical trials have assessed the efficacy of novel agents in mCRPC as monotherapy or combination therapy, including ARPIs, chemotherapy, PARPIs, and radioligand therapy.9,55,56 However, clinical outcomes of mCRPC remain unsatisfactory, and the treatment for mCRPC still need further exploration due to heterogeneity and treatment resistance following disease progression.57 Thus, exploring novel effective approaches for mCRPC remains a critical clinical need.

Immunotherapy, especially ICI, has developed as a promising therapeutic option across various malignancies, such as melanoma, lung cancer, and genitourinary tumors.58–60 However, the role of ICI in mCRPC has been less defined, and clinical trials evaluating ICI in mCRPC have yielded inconsistent and even contradictory results, which may reflect the complicated interplay between tumor biology, immune microenvironment, and previous treatments. Meanwhile, the immunosuppressive nature of the PCa partly inhibits the therapeutic response to ICI, which has get more attention recently.61 Moreover, most clinical trials evaluating ICI in mCRPC have been limited by small sample sizes, heterogeneous patient populations and combination protocols, which may decrease the robustness of conclusions.

In this meta-analysis, we firstly pooled the estimates of efficacy and safety, and found significant heterogeneity and publication biases between studies. Significant between-study heterogeneity may attribute to different design, treatment protocols and patient populations. Thus, we try to address these issues by using subgroup meta-analysis and reconstructed IPD. This methodology allowed us to mitigate the considerable heterogeneity observed across included studies and to evaluate specific therapeutic subgroups and treatment regimens more accurately. Our meta-analysis revealed pooled 1-y OS of 63% and 6-month PFS of 41%, reflecting moderate survival benefits. Further subgroup analyses and IPD analyses demonstrated different efficacy of treatment regimens, in which combination of ICI plus other agents showed more OS and PFS benefits comparing with mono and dual ICI therapy. Specifically, combining ICI with vaccine and chemotherapy may provide more OS and PFS benefits respectively, highlighting the potential of ICI combination therapy in mCRPC.

Reconstructed IPD also enabled us to compare survival outcomes in the setting of different ICI agents and therapeutic targets. Ipilimumab treatment showed better tendency on OS in the overall population comparing with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors atezolizumab, nivolumab and pembrolizumab. However, this tendency was derived from small, early phase and single-arm trials for selected patients. These observations were therefore hypothesis-generating, and do not establish ipilimumab as superior to PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected mCRPC. Therefore, these findings should be interpreted with considerable caution rather than definitive evidence that ipilimumab improves OS in unselected mCRPC. Larger RCT, particularly in biomarker-selected patients, are needed to clarify whether any subgroup derives durable OS benefit from CTLA-4 blockade.

Interestingly, nivolumab group exhibited the favorable signal on PFS, indicating potential effects of PD-1 blockade on short-term disease control. We explored the efficacy of different ICI therapeutic targets and found that CTLA-4 inhibitors may offer longer OS comparing with PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors or dual ICI strategies. These findings align with previous viewpoints on distinct mechanisms of immune activation between CTLA-4 and PD-1 pathways, which may potentially influence their efficacy in mCRPC.13 Additionally, meta-regression analyses found ICI therapeutic targets significantly contributed to heterogeneity, suggesting that the choice of immune checkpoint target may impact clinical outcomes. These results underscore the importance of selecting appropriate ICI strategies to maximize therapeutic efficacy.

Notably, dual checkpoint blockade with CTLA-4 plus PD-1 may increase anti-tumor immunity but is consistently associated with a substantially higher toxicity burden than single ICI. In our pooled estimate, regimens combining ipilimumab with nivolumab or other ICIs exhibited high rates of grade ≥3 TRAEs and irAEs, including colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies and treatment discontinuations. These observations are in line with the results from other tumor types and underscore that any potential gain from CTLA-4 plus PD-1 combinations must be carefully balanced against the risk of immune-mediated events.62 In clinical practice, the use of dual ICI in mCRPC should weight the risks and benefits, and require multidisciplinary monitoring to promptly detect and manage immune-related toxicities.

In this meta-analysis, only one phase III RCT was included. In this IMbassador250 study, combination of atezolizumab plus enzalutamide did not improve OS comparing with enzalutamide alone in mCRPC patients who had progressed on abiraterone (HR: 1.12, 95% CI: 0.91–1.37; P = .28). Although the ORR was numerically higher with the combination (13.7% vs 7.4%), rPFS (HR: 0.90, 95% CI 0.75–1.07) and time to PSA progression (HR: 1.04, 95% CI 0.87–1.24) were not different between arms.These neutral results in an unselected mCRPC population are consistent with our pooled estimates for atezolizumab. Thus, this combination regimen do not yet provide a clear OS benefit for unselected mCRPC.

Current evidence suggests that MSI-H/dMMR and TMB-high status, as well as DDR alterations such as BRCA1/2, ATM and CDK12 biallelic loss, may identify the subsets of mCRPC more likely to respond to ICI.10–12,20,21 From a design perspective, our findings support several directions for future ICI study in mCRPC. Given the approval of ICIs in men with MSI-H, dMMR, high TMB, strategies basing on biomarker enrichment should be incorporated into randomized studies. Meanwhile, combinations with established treatments, such as PARPIs, novel ARPIs and radioligand therapy, may offer more biologically rational backbones and warrant rigorous testing in powered trials. Future research should also focus on rational combinations of ICI with existing life-prolonging therapies and well-designed randomized trials to define the subset of patients who truly benefit from ICI regimens.

Additionally, future biomarker-related studies should stratify randomization by key clinical and molecular variables, including prior exposure to ARPIs and ctDNA-defined alterations in DDR genes, CDK12 and other candidate predictors of immune sensitivity. Embedding serial ctDNA analysis into study may also help to elucidate dynamic resistance mechanisms and identify early markers of benefit or failure during ICI therapy.

This meta-analysis has several limitations that should be considered. Firstly, the variations in design, patient selection, treatment protocol, combination therapy, and outcome reporting across the included clinical trials inevitably introduce heterogeneity. Particularly, almost all included trials were single-arm or non-comparative phase I/II studies, with only one phase III RCT available, which may bias the pooled estimates and results. Although we employed subgroup and meta-regression analyses to address this issue, residual heterogeneity still exists. Thus, all results of this study should be explained carefully. Secondly, our approach of reconstructing IPD from published Kaplan-Meier curves could not entirely simulate original patient-level data, potentially introducing inaccuracy in survival estimates. Lastly, publication biases were detected in several pooled analyses, which impacted the robustness of pooled efficacy and safety. Thus, some pooled findings of our meta-analysis should be interpreted with caution, and future large-scale RCTs with standardized protocols are essential to confirm these findings.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In unselected mCRPC patients, the overall survival outcomes with ICI remain variable. This meta-analysis provided encouraging but preliminary signals that combination therapy with ICI may provide more survival benefits than mono or dual ICI therapy. Particularly, ICI plus chemotherapy or vaccine may offer greater benefit than other combination regimens. Given that most available evidence derives from small, early phase and non-randomized studies, all results should be viewed as hypothesis-generating and explained carefully. Future large randomized trials are needed to validate the combination regimens with ICI in mCRPC, especially among biomarker-selected patients.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: Authors appreciate all investigators and participants of included trials. Authors thank for the assistance from Dr. Xusheng Zhu (UCL) at UK.

(I) Conception and design: Fuxun Zhang; Qiang Fu; Wei Zhang; Geng Zhang; (II) Administrative support: Uzoamaka Adaobi Okoli; Geng Zhang; (III) Provision of study materials or patients: Fuxun Zhang; Zhirong Luo; Qi Xue; Yang Xiong; Lei Wang; (IV) Collection and assembly of data: Fuxun Zhang; Zhirong Luo; Qi Xue; Yang Xiong; Pati-Alam Alisha; (V) Data analysis and interpretation: Fuxun Zhang; Zhirong Luo; Qi Xue; Wei Zhang; Lei Wang; Yang Xiong; (VI) Manuscript writing: Fuxun Zhang; Zhirong Luo; Pati-Alam Alisha; Uzoamaka Adaobi Okoli; (VII) Final approval of manuscript: Fuxun Zhang; Yang Xiong; Zhirong Luo; Qi Xue; Lei Wang; Qiang Fu; Wei Zhang; Pati-Alam Alisha; Uzoamaka Adaobi Okoli; Geng Zhang.
All named authors meet the ICMJE criteria for authorship in this article, take responsibility for the integrity of the work as a whole, and have given their approval for this version to be published.

bio.p: The clinical and research focus of corresponding author Geng Zhang is kidney transplantation and urological tumor therapy. He has particular expertise in autologous kidney transplantation for complex urologic disease, surgical management of post-transplant ureteral obstruction, and donor organ purification. He serves on national and regional committees, including member of the Kidney Transplantation Group of the Organ Transplantation Branch of the Chinese Medical Association; standing committee member and Deputy Secretary-General of the Transplant Technology Branch of the Chinese Society for Medical Biotechnology. Co-corresponding author Uzoamaka Adaobi Okoli focuses on the 3D model of prostate cancer, and has rich experience in the radiotherapy of prostate cancer.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: The authors have no relevant affiliations or financial involvement with any organization or entity with a financial interest in or financial conflict with the subject matter or materials discussed in the manuscript. This includes employment, consultancies, honoraria, stock ownership or options, expert testimony, grants or patents received or pending, or royalties.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: The datasets analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: The ethical approval and patient consent were not needed. No human participants or animals were involved in this study.

sec.title: Supplementary material
sec.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/21645515.2026.2626084
